title: Thai Iced Tea
description: Experience the vibrant taste of Thailand with this classic Thai Iced Tea (Cha Yen). This refreshing beverage boasts a unique blend of strong black tea, often spiced, sweetened to perfection, and crowned with a luscious swirl of evaporated and condensed milk, creating an irresistibly creamy and aromatic delight perfect for any hot day.

ingredients.notes: or to taste, for sweetness and creaminess
or to taste, for richness and color

instructions.title: Brew the Tea Base
instructions.text: Bring 4 cups of filtered water to a rolling boil. In a heatproof pitcher or large teapot, add the Thai tea mix. If using, add the star anise pods and lightly crushed cardamom pods. Pour the hot water over the tea leaves and spices.

instructions.title: Steep and Strain
instructions.text: Let the tea steep for 10-15 minutes. For a stronger brew, you can steep slightly longer, but be careful not to over-steep, which can introduce bitterness. Using a fine-mesh strainer or a traditional Thai tea filter bag, strain the tea into another clean pitcher, pressing down on the leaves to extract maximum flavor. Discard the tea leaves and spices.

instructions.title: Sweeten and Chill
instructions.text: While the tea is still hot, add the granulated sugar to the strained tea. Stir well until the sugar is completely dissolved. Taste and adjust sweetness if desired. Allow the sweetened tea to cool down to room temperature, then chill thoroughly in the refrigerator for at least 1-2 hours, or until very cold. This step is crucial for a truly refreshing drink.

instructions.title: Assemble and Serve
instructions.text: Fill four tall glasses generously with ice. Pour the chilled sweetened Thai tea into each glass, filling about 3/4 of the way. Slowly pour about 2-3 tablespoons of sweetened condensed milk into each glass, followed by 2-3 tablespoons of evaporated milk. For a beautiful layered effect, pour the milks gently over the back of a spoon.

instructions.title: Enjoy
instructions.text: Serve immediately. Stir gently before drinking to combine the layers and enjoy the authentic taste of Thai Iced Tea!

tips: For the true authentic flavor, invest in a quality Thai tea mix, often labeled 'Cha Yen' or 'Number One Brand.' Its unique blend of black tea, spices, and food coloring gives it its characteristic taste and vibrant orange hue.

The amount of sugar, sweetened condensed milk, and evaporated milk can be adjusted to your personal preference. Add more condensed milk for a sweeter, richer taste, or more evaporated milk for creaminess without extra sweetness.

For that iconic layered look, pour the milks slowly over the back of a spoon onto the tea. They will float on top, creating a beautiful visual before stirring. This also allows the drinker to mix to their preferred creaminess.

You can brew and sweeten the tea base a day or two in advance and keep it chilled in the refrigerator. This allows the flavors to meld and ensures a perfectly cold drink when ready to serve. Just add ice and milks right before serving.
article:
## The Refreshing History of Cha Yen: Thailand's Iconic Brew
Thai Iced Tea, or 'Cha Yen' as it's known in Thailand, is far more than just a sweet drink; it's a cultural icon. While the exact origins are debated, tea drinking was likely introduced to Thailand by Chinese immigrants and Indian traders centuries ago. However, the distinctly creamy, sweet, and spiced version we adore today began to emerge in the mid-20th century. As Thailand modernized and international influences grew, the simple black tea evolved. Street vendors, ever innovative, started adding local spices like star anise and tamarind, along with the readily available condensed and evaporated milks, which were becoming popular preservatives and flavor enhancers. The vibrant orange hue, often achieved with food coloring, became a signature, making the drink instantly recognizable. From bustling Bangkok street stalls to upscale restaurants, Cha Yen quickly cemented its place as Thailand's quintessential refreshing beverage, a testament to its delicious adaptability and widespread appeal.

## The Magic Behind the Blend: Why Thai Iced Tea Works
The allure of Thai Iced Tea lies in its masterful balance of strong tea, sweet richness, and aromatic spices. The foundation is a specific Thai tea mix, typically a strong black tea that's often blended with star anise, tamarind seed, and sometimes orange blossom water, giving it a unique, complex flavor profile that stands up beautifully to the sweetness and creaminess. The vibrant orange color, though often enhanced by food coloring, is part of its visual charm. The use of both sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk is crucial. Sweetened condensed milk provides intense sweetness and a thick, velvety texture, while evaporated milk adds a deeper, richer creaminess without overwhelming the tea's flavor with additional sugar. This dual-milk approach creates a depth of flavor and mouthfeel that plain milk cannot achieve. Finally, serving it over abundant ice is non-negotiable; the cold temperature cuts through the richness, making it incredibly refreshing and perfectly suited for Thailand's tropical climate.

## Chef's Secrets for the Perfect Cha Yen
Achieving an authentic and truly delicious Thai Iced Tea goes beyond just mixing ingredients. Firstly, the quality of your Thai tea mix is paramount. Invest in a reputable brand like 'Number One Brand' for that unmistakable flavor. While some recipes suggest adding extra star anise or cardamom, a good quality mix already contains these notes, so taste before adding more. Secondly, the steeping time is critical. Aim for 10-15 minutes; over-steeping can extract too much tannin, leading to a bitter brew that even the milks can't fully mask. Thirdly, ensure your tea base is thoroughly chilled before assembling. Pouring hot or even lukewarm tea over ice will dilute the drink too quickly. For the iconic layered presentation, pour the milks slowly over the back of a spoon onto the tea-filled glass. This creates a beautiful visual and allows the drinker to stir and adjust the creaminess to their liking. Lastly, don't be afraid to adjust the sweetness and creaminess to your personal preference; the amounts provided are a guideline, but the perfect Cha Yen is ultimately tailored to your palate.

## Serving Suggestions and Creative Variations
Thai Iced Tea is a versatile beverage that can be enjoyed in numerous ways. Traditionally, it's served in a tall glass, brimming with ice, with the distinct layers of tea and milk creating an inviting visual. It pairs wonderfully with spicy Thai dishes, such as Pad Kee Mao, Green Curry, or Som Tum, as its sweetness and creaminess offer a perfect counterpoint to the heat. It also makes a delightful accompaniment to rich desserts or simply as a refreshing pick-me-up on a warm afternoon. For those looking to experiment, several variations exist. A popular alternative is Thai Iced Coffee (Oliang), prepared similarly but using a specific blend of coffee beans, corn, soybeans, and sesame seeds. For a dairy-free option, coconut condensed milk and full-fat coconut milk can be used to create a vegan Thai Iced Tea that's equally rich and satisfying. Adventurous mixologists might even consider infusing the tea base with a splash of dark rum or spiced vodka for an adult twist on this beloved classic.
